expanded_description: Comprehensive synthesis of two-dimensional area calculations, circular regions, three-dimensional surface area and volume formulas, scale factor relationships, composite solids, and real-world density applications

core_concept.principle: Area and volume are additive measures of two- and three-dimensional space that scale predictably with linear dimensions, enabling systematic calculation of simple, composite, and similar figures
core_concept.explanation: Two-dimensional area measures the surface coverage of a shape, while three-dimensional volume measures the space enclosed within a solid. Both rely on fundamental base formulas (triangles, rectangles, circles) that extend through decomposition, subtraction, or extrusion/revolution into higher dimensions. When figures are similar, linear scaling by factor k affects area by k² and volume by k³. Mastery of these relationships bridges pure geometry with engineering, architecture, manufacturing, and scientific measurement.

image.alt: Diagram showing 1D length scaling to 2D area (k²) and 3D volume (k³)
image.caption: Figure 1: Dimensionality determines scaling power: lengths → k, areas → k², volumes → k³

sections.introduction: Complex polygons and irregular shapes can be accurately measured by breaking them into familiar components or subtracting missing regions from bounding rectangles.

key_concept.definition: The area of any composite figure equals the sum of its non-overlapping parts, or the area of a larger containing shape minus the areas of removed sections
key_concept.context: This additive/subtractive property works because area is a measure of planar coverage that partitions without gaps or overlaps

image.alt: L-shaped polygon divided into two rectangles with dashed lines
image.caption: Figure 2: Decompose complex shapes into basic polygons or use subtraction method

principles.statement: If a region is partitioned into non-overlapping subregions, the total area equals the sum of the subregion areas
principles.application: Break irregular boundaries into rectangles, triangles, and trapezoids; calculate each; then sum
principles.example: An L-shaped garden can be split into a 4m×6m rectangle and a 2m×3m rectangle, totaling 30 m²
principles.analogy: Like assembling a puzzle—each piece has a known area, and together they cover the exact total space

worked_examples.title: Composite Figure with Missing Section
worked_examples.problem: A rectangular metal plate measures 18 cm by 12 cm. A right triangle with legs 6 cm and 4 cm is cut from one corner. Find the remaining area of the plate.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Subtractive area method for composite regions

sections.introduction: Circular regions require proportional reasoning because area and arc length scale directly with the central angle relative to a full 360° rotation.

key_concept.definition: A sector's area is $\frac{\theta}{360^\circ} \pi r^2$ and its arc length is $\frac{\theta}{360^\circ} (2\pi r)$, where $\theta$ is the central angle in degrees
key_concept.context: These formulas treat circles as continuous proportional systems: every degree represents 1/360 of the total circumference and area

image.alt: Circle with highlighted sector showing central angle theta and radius r
image.caption: Figure 3: Sector area and arc length are proportional fractions of the whole circle

categories.description: Region bounded by two radii and an intercepted arc
categories.detailed_mechanism: Scale total circle area by the ratio of central angle to 360°

examples.explanation: A 60° slice of a 12-inch diameter pizza covers 1/6 of the total area

image.alt: Pizza circle with 60 degree sector shaded
image.caption: Figure 4: Sector area formula applies to any wedge-shaped region

categories.description: Region bounded by a chord and its intercepted arc
categories.detailed_mechanism: Subtract the triangular portion from the sector area: $A_{segment} = A_{sector} - A_{triangle}$

examples.explanation: Cross-sectional water area equals sector minus isosceles triangle formed by radii

image.caption: Figure 5: Segment area requires two-step subtraction

worked_examples.title: Shaded Region: Square Minus Quarter Circle
worked_examples.problem: A square has side length 10 cm. A quarter circle with radius 10 cm is drawn from one corner, overlapping the square. Find the area of the unshaded region inside the square but outside the quarter circle. Round to nearest tenth.

step_by_step.explanation: Use sector formula with $\theta = 90^\circ$ or simply $\frac{1}{4}\pi r^2$

sections.introduction: Three-dimensional measurements extend 2D base formulas through height, slant height, or radius. Distinguishing lateral from total surface area is critical for real-world material estimation.

key_concept.definition: Volume of prisms/cylinders: $V = Bh$; Volume of pyramids/cones: $V = \frac{1}{3}Bh$; Sphere volume: $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$. Surface area sums all exposed faces, with lateral SA excluding bases.
key_concept.context: The 1/3 factor for pyramids/cones reflects how volume concentrates toward the apex, occupying exactly one-third of the bounding prism/cylinder volume

image.alt: Grid showing prism, cylinder, pyramid, cone, sphere with labeled formulas
image.caption: Figure 6: Core 3D formulas derive from base area and height relationships

process_flow.process: Identify solid type and given dimensions
process_flow.description: Determine if figure is prism, cylinder, pyramid, cone, or sphere; extract radius, diameter, height, slant height
Select lateral SA, total SA, or volume based on problem requirements
Compute B (circle, polygon), plug into formulas
Compute final answer, ensure area in square units, volume in cubic units
process_flow.organization: Volume ∝ length³, Area ∝ length²; mismatch indicates formula error

worked_examples.title: Cone Surface Area and Volume
worked_examples.problem: A right circular cone has radius 6 cm and slant height 10 cm. Find its total surface area and volume. Round to nearest tenth.

step_by_step.step: Find cone height using Pythagorean Theorem

worked_examples.concept_applied: Cone formulas with Pythagorean height derivation

sections.introduction: When solids maintain identical shape but differ in size, their measurements relate through predictable powers of the linear scale factor k.

principles.statement: For similar solids with linear scale factor k: linear ratio = k, surface area ratio = k², volume ratio = k³
principles.application: Given any one ratio, solve for k (square root for area, cube root for volume) to find missing measurements
principles.example: If two spheres have volume ratio 8:27, then $k^3 = \frac{8}{27} \Rightarrow k = \frac{2}{3}$. Surface area ratio = $(\frac{2}{3})^2 = \frac{4}{9}$
principles.analogy: Like resizing a 3D model: doubling dimensions makes it 4× heavier in surface coating, but 8× heavier in material

worked_examples.title: Finding Unknown Volume from Surface Area Ratio
worked_examples.problem: Two similar rectangular prisms have surface area ratio 25:64. The smaller prism has volume 150 cm³. Find the volume of the larger prism.

step_by_step.explanation: Surface area ratio = k², so take square root

worked_examples.final_answer: The larger prism has volume 614.4 cm³
worked_examples.concept_applied: Converting area ratio to linear scale, then to volume ratio

sections.section_title: Composite Solids, Holes, and Density Applications
sections.introduction: Real-world objects combine multiple solids, contain hollow regions, or require mass calculations using material density. Volume remains strictly additive/subtractive, while surface area requires careful hidden-face accounting.

categories.description: Multiple solids joined without overlapping interiors
categories.detailed_mechanism: Sum individual volumes. For surface area, add individual SAs then subtract 2× each overlapping face area

examples.explanation: Volume = cone + hemisphere. SA = lateral cone + curved hemisphere (base hidden)

image.alt: Cone with hemisphere on top showing hidden base
image.caption: Figure 7: Combined volumes add; hidden surfaces subtract from SA

categories.description: Solid with another shape drilled or cut out
categories.detailed_mechanism: Volume = original - removed. Surface area = original - 2× openings + interior lateral area of hole

examples.explanation: Subtract cylinder volume; subtract 2 circular faces but ADD cylinder lateral area $2\pi rh$

image.caption: Figure 8: Holes create new exposed interior surface

key_concept.definition: Density $\rho = \frac{m}{V}$ connects geometric volume to physical mass, enabling material selection, shipping calculations, and buoyancy analysis
key_concept.context: Rearranging gives $m = \rho V$ (find weight before construction) and $V = \frac{m}{\rho}$ (find storage needs from material mass)

image.alt: Triangle showing density, mass, volume relationships
image.caption: Figure 9: Density bridges geometry and physics for real-world engineering

worked_examples.title: Composite Solid Volume and Mass Calculation
worked_examples.problem: A machine part consists of a steel cylinder (radius 4 cm, height 10 cm) with a hemispherical cap on top. Steel density = 7.85 g/cm³. Find the total volume and mass of the part. Round mass to nearest gram.
worked_examples.final_answer: Total volume ≈ 636.7 cm³; Mass ≈ 4998 grams (≈ 5.0 kg)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Composite volume summation and density-mass conversion

summary: Area and volume calculations rely on fundamental base formulas that extend through decomposition, subtraction, and dimensional scaling. Two-dimensional area measures planar coverage using triangles, rectangles, and circles, with composite shapes solved by adding non-overlapping parts or subtracting cutouts. Circular regions use proportional reasoning: sector area and arc length scale with $\frac{\theta}{360^\circ}$ of the full circle. Three-dimensional solids extend these principles: prisms and cylinders use $V=Bh$ and $SA = LA + \text{bases}$, while pyramids and cones introduce the $\frac{1}{3}$ factor and slant height. Spheres follow unique formulas $SA = 4\pi r^2$ and $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$. When solids are similar with linear scale factor k, areas scale by k² and volumes by k³, enabling rapid prediction of missing measurements. Composite 3D figures require strict additive volume logic and careful hidden-surface accounting for SA, while holes subtract material but add interior lateral area. Density $\rho = \frac{m}{V}$ bridges geometry and physics, allowing engineers to calculate mass from volume, optimize material selection, solve shipping constraints, and analyze buoyancy. Mastery requires systematic identification of figure type, correct formula selection, dimensional consistency, and strategic use of scale relationships across 2D and 3D contexts.
